Browser Sudoku game banner with a Sudoku grid, controls, and article title.A small update from Games I Know — and what we learned while making Sudoku simple, calm, and easy to play online.
We recently added a new game to Games I Know: a free online Sudoku puzzle.
The idea is simple.
Open the game, choose a difficulty, fill the 9x9 grid, and solve the puzzle using logic.
No login.
No download.
No app install.
Just open the browser and play.
You can try it here:
https://gamesiknow.com/games/sudoku/
Why we chose Sudoku
Sudoku is one of those games that almost everyone understands, but it still gives the player a real challenge.
It does not need fast reactions.
It does not need multiplayer.
It does not need a long tutorial.
The rules are simple:

• each row needs numbers 1 to 9

• each column needs numbers 1 to 9

• each 3x3 box needs numbers 1 to 9

• no number should repeat in the same row, column, or box
That makes Sudoku a good fit for Games I Know.
It is simple enough for casual players, but still challenging enough to keep people engaged.
We wanted the game to feel calm
One of our main goals was to make the game feel calm and easy to start.
A lot of games today ask users to create an account, download an app, accept too many prompts, or go through too many screens before playing.
We wanted the opposite.
The flow should feel like this:
Open page → choose difficulty → start solvingThat is it.
For a browser puzzle game, this matters a lot. If the first few seconds feel heavy, many users will leave before even trying the game.
Difficulty levels make the game more welcoming
Sudoku can become frustrating if the puzzle is too hard too quickly.
So we added three difficulty levels:

• Easy

• Medium

• Hard
Easy is better for beginners or quick breaks.
Medium is better for players who already know the rules.
Hard is better for people who want a slower and more focused puzzle.
This helps different types of players enjoy the same game in their own way.
Notes mode was important
For Sudoku, notes mode is not just a small extra feature. It is part of how many people solve the puzzle.
Sometimes a cell has multiple possible answers.
For example, a cell might be:
2, 5, or 8Notes allow the player to keep track of those possibilities without guessing.
As the puzzle progresses, the player can remove candidates and slowly reach the correct answer.
That makes the game feel more logical and more satisfying.
Mobile experience matters
Sudoku is easy to design for desktop.
There is more space.
The keyboard is available.
The board is easier to scan.
Mobile is different.
The grid must be readable.
The buttons must be easy to tap.
The selected cell must be clear.
Notes mode should not feel confusing.
The user should not need to zoom.
A lot of casual players open games from their phone, so mobile support is not optional.
Small details improve the experience
We also added small feedback elements to make the game easier to follow:

• timer

• mistake counter

• selected cell state

• notes mode

• clear cell option

• keyboard support

• number controls for touch users
These details are small, but they help the player understand what is happening without making the game feel noisy.
The goal is to keep the experience simple, but not empty.
Why browser games still make sense
Many games today push people toward app stores.
But we still believe browser games have a place.
A browser game can be opened instantly from a link.
It can be played on desktop, mobile, or tablet.
It can be shared easily.
It does not ask for installation.
It does not require an account.
That simplicity is powerful, especially for casual games.
Someone can play during a short break, share it with a friend, or open it from a blog post without committing to anything.
The SEO side also matters
For Games I Know, we do not want to depend only on social posts.
Each game needs a proper page that users and search engines can understand.
For Sudoku, we have both:
https://gamesiknow.com/games/sudoku/
and a beginner-friendly guide:
https://gamesiknow.com/blog/play-sudoku-online-free/
The game page is for playing.
The blog post explains the rules, difficulty levels, tips, and how to play.
That gives users more context and also helps the game become easier to discover.
A blank game screen is not enough if you want long-term organic traffic.
What we learned
Building a Sudoku game is not only about creating a valid puzzle.
You also have to think about:

• how fast users can start playing

• how beginners understand the rules

• how difficulty affects frustration

• how notes mode supports real solving

• how mobile controls feel

• how much feedback the player needs

• how the game page fits into SEO

• how to keep the experience simple
Small browser games still need thoughtful product decisions.
Try the game
You can play the Sudoku game here:
https://gamesiknow.com/games/sudoku/
We also wrote a beginner-friendly guide here:
https://gamesiknow.com/blog/play-sudoku-online-free/
We are still improving the game, so feedback is welcome.
What would you add next to an online Sudoku game?
